title: What is the meaning of the live action footage in EoE
display_title: What is the meaning of the live action footage in EoE
wikitext:
[]: What is the meaning of the live-action footage in EoE?             Quote Post by TheMarauder » Sun Jan 29, 2012 8:04 am I like to think (delusionally so) that I understood most of End of Evangelion, but the one thing I can't seem to figure out is what the purpose/meaning of the live action footage is. I searched the forums for any discussion about this previously but couldn't seem to find anything, although there probably has been and I just missed it. Also if I have broken any sacred rules or violated the Dead Sea Scrolls Apocrypha I apologize, this is literally my first post on these forums.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Sun Jan 29, 2012 8:19 am Short answer: THE FOURTH WALL

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Jan 29, 2012 8:37 am The live-action sequence is roughly analogous to the trippier bits of the final TV episode, where Shinji is figuring out this reality stuff for himself. And rather than have a diversion into a world where everything is a normal school anime series, this time we have a more prosaic alternate world to look at. The live action sequence that was shot but not included in the final work was even more explicitly an alternate world, one without Shinji.

[]:             Quote Post by Ceimoa Nan » Sun Jan 29, 2012 2:47 pm What Shinji does with Instrumentality is the extreme of what he did throughout most of the series, run away. If life became too hard for him, he would go into his room, listen to his SDAT, and close himself off from the rest of the world. During Instrumentality, he did the same thing, by making everyone cease to exist, or merging everyone together (which, I guess, is the same thing?), and in the process creating alternate realities like we see in Episode 26 and the deleted live action sequence. During Instrumentality, Shinji realizes that this isn't a good thing, and he needs to deal with the "reality" of A.T. Fields making interaction with others difficult. What's special about the live action sequence we see in the film is that it reminds the audience that, as the kind of people who are into this mecha anime stuff, they're guilty of the same things Shinji is. The first shot is of an empty theater with a Rei doll barley visible on one step. We see images of a city, a cat, power lines, trains, a swing set. Then we see the voice actors for Misato, Asuka, and Rei in a crowded street, first looking towards the camera in casual clothes (representing reality?) and then facing away in costume (representing false reality?). And then we go back to a movie theater, crowded with Eva fans. One has an Asuka and a Rei doll, another gestures towards the camera. We are asked, "Do you feel good?" A mirror is held up to the audience, kind of asking, "don't you all feel like losers?" Then we see love and hate letters sent to Gainax in response to the TV series ending (or was it Death and Rebirth?), showing how invested fans got into this false reality. I'm guessing that footage was taken during a premiere of Death and Rebirth. Either that or they took it 30 minutes before EoE aired, and then put it into the film really really quick. What I find hard to keep track of was the difference between reality, truth, false reality, and dreams. It's like, first Rei is saying dreams are bad, then they're good and only false realities are bad. Or there's no truth, only reality. Or was it that there is only truth, and no reality? (The truth/reality thing is focused on more in EoTV.) Maybe the Japanese words translated into "truth" and "reality" have slightly different meanings than truth and reality. The take away seemed to be that dreams are good, but only if others are involved. And, applying it to the audience, it means that being an obsessive fan is good, but only if you don't use it for escapism. This thing where there's a message to the protagonist that parallels a message to the audience reminds me of the ending of Lost. SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by Trajan » Sun Jan 29, 2012 9:42 pm Ask five different people, and you'll get five different answers. I personally think Anno was just trying to re-do the alternate reality scenes in 26 with a bit more of an avant-garde style: basically being artsy for arts sake.

Article: What is the meaning of the live action footage in EoE
 interaction with others difficult. What's special about the live action sequence we see in the film is that it reminds the audience that, as the kind of people who are into this mecha anime stuff, they're guilty of the same things Shinji is. The first shot is of an empty theater with a Rei doll barley visible on one step. We see images of a city, a cat, power lines, trains, a swing set. Then we see the voice actors for Misato, Asuka, and Rei in a crowded street, first looking towards the camera in casual clothes (representing reality?) and then facing away in costume (representing false reality?). And then we go back to a movie theater, crowded with Eva fans. One has an Asuka and a Rei doll, another gestures towards the camera. We are asked, "Do you feel good?" A mirror is held up to the audience, kind of asking, "don't you all feel like losers?" Then we see love and hate letters sent to Gainax in response to the TV series ending (or was it Death and Rebirth?), showing how invested fans got into this false reality. I'm guessing that footage was taken during a premiere of Death and Rebirth. Either that or they took it 30 minutes before EoE aired, and then put it into the film really really quick. What I find hard to keep track of was the difference between reality, truth, false reality, and dreams. It's like, first Rei is saying dreams are bad, then they're good and only false realities are bad. Or there's no truth, only reality. Or was it that there is only truth, and no reality? (The truth/reality thing is focused on more in EoTV.) Maybe the Japanese words translated into "truth" and "reality" have slightly different meanings than truth and reality. The take away seemed to be that dreams are good, but only if others are involved. And, applying it to the audience, it means that being an obsessive fan is good, but only if you don't use it for escapism. This thing where there's a message to the protagonist that parallels a message to the audience reminds me of the ending of Lost. SPOILER: Show
